coredata.dc:description: This study presents the synthesis of fluorescent toner carbon dots (TCDs) emitting cyan-blue fluorescence via a hydrothermal approach using waste toner powder. The TCDs demonstrated selective fluorescence quenching in response to Hg2+ ions, which could be fully restored using an EDTA-saturated solution, enabling a reversible and sustainable detection system. The TCDs exhibited excellent linearity over a broad Hg2+ detection range (0–500 µM), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 17.3 nM. To enhance real-world applicability, a low-cost, portable, and recyclable TCD-based paper sensor was developed for naked-eye detection of Hg2+. When integrated with a smartphone-assisted color analysis platform, the sensor enabled quantitative detection of Hg2+ (0–50 µM) with an LOD of 180 nM. The paper sensor retained its functionality for at least four detection cycles, contributing to sustainability and resource conservation. Furthermore, oxygen-containing functional groups played a crucial role in Hg2+ detection sensitivity. Owing to their high fluorescence quantum efficiency, TCDs were successfully applied as fluorescent invisible ink for anti-counterfeiting (AC) applications, with images developed using screen printing, brush mode, and pen mode. Additionally, latent fingerprints (LFPs) developed using TCDs on various substrates displayed well-defined Level 1–3 fingerprint details, confirming their efficacy for forensic applications. These findings highlight TCDs as a multifunctional fluorescence-based sensor for Hg2+ detection, security labelling and LFP analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study presents the synthesis of fluorescent toner carbon dots (TCDs) emitting cyan-blue fluorescence via a hydrothermal approach using waste toner powder. The TCDs demonstrated selective fluorescence quenching in response to Hg2+ ions, which could be fully restored using an EDTA-saturated solution, enabling a reversible and sustainable detection system. The TCDs exhibited excellent linearity over a broad Hg2+ detection range (0–500 µM), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 17.3 nM. To enhance real-world applicability, a low-cost, portable, and recyclable TCD-based paper sensor was developed for naked-eye detection of Hg2+. When integrated with a smartphone-assisted color analysis platform, the sensor enabled quantitative detection of Hg2+ (0–50 µM) with an LOD of 180 nM. The paper sensor retained its functionality for at least four detection cycles, contributing to sustainability and resource conservation. Furthermore, oxygen-containing functional groups played a crucial role in Hg2+ detection sensitivity. Owing to their high fluorescence quantum efficiency, TCDs were successfully applied as fluorescent invisible ink for anti-counterfeiting (AC) applications, with images developed using screen printing, brush mode, and pen mode. Additionally, latent fingerprints (LFPs) developed using TCDs on various substrates displayed well-defined Level 1–3 fingerprint details, confirming their efficacy for forensic applications. These findings highlight TCDs as a multifunctional fluorescence-based sensor for Hg2+ detection, security labelling and LFP analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions are among the most hazardous and pervasive environmental pollutants. Mercury(II) ion (Hg²⁺), a representative heavy metal, is particularly concerning due to its non-biodegradable nature and strong tendency for bioaccumulation, posing a significant threat to both ecosystems and human health. The toxicity of Hg²⁺ arises from its high affinity for thiol groups in intracellular proteins and enzymes, which disrupts normal biological functions and leads to severe physiological disorders [1,2]. In recent decades, human activities like mining, coal combustion, industrial processes, and waste incineration have led to widespread mercury pollution [3]. Mercury exposure can occur through contaminated water, food, or everyday products [4], and its strong binding to thiol groups in proteins disrupts cellular functions, causing cognitive and neurological harm [5]. Thus, developing fast, reliable methods for real-time Hg²⁺ detection is vital for environmental and health safety.

While ratiometric fluorescence probes offer significant analytical advantages, including enhanced resolution and internal calibration, quenching-based sensors remain widely used due to their simplicity and cost-effectiveness. Ratiometric systems measure the intensity ratio of two emission bands, which allows them to self-correct for fluctuations in probe concentration, excitation intensity, and environmental conditions, thereby improving reproducibility and sensitivity [6,7]. These characteristics make ratiometric probes particularly suitable for quantitative analyses and on-site sensing applications using portable devices. However, the design and synthesis of dual-emission ratiometric systems can be more complex and resource-intensive. In contrast, quenching-based sensors rely on the reduction or suppression of fluorescence intensity upon analyte binding. This mechanism allows for straightforward sensor fabrication and visual signal output, which is advantageous for rapid, qualitative screening. Despite their relatively lower resolution compared to ratiometric systems, quenching-based approaches remain highly effective when designed for strong selectivity as demonstrated in the present study for Hg²⁺ detection. Thus, the chosen quenching strategy using TCDs provides an optimal balance between simplicity, cost, and practical field applicability, while maintaining high specificity for Hg²⁺ ions [8].

Given the urgency of mercury monitoring, various analytical methods have been developed, including CV-AAS [9], ICP-AES [10], fluorescence spectroscopy [11], colorimetric assays [12], and SERS [13]. While effective, these methods often involve costly equipment, complex procedures, and lengthy processing. To overcome these issues, water-soluble fluorescent probes and spirogyra-based compounds have been synthesized for detecting heavy metals in aqueous samples [14]. Metal nanoparticles like AgNPs and AuNPs are also widely used due to their low cost and eco-friendly nature [15]. Colorimetric techniques are especially attractive for their simplicity and visual detection capability without advanced instruments [16]. Nevertheless, current probes face limitations, including complex synthesis, low solubility, slow response, and interference issues. For bioimaging, highly soluble probes are needed to avoid organic solvents, yet successful Hg²⁺ imaging remains rare [17], underscoring the need for sensitive, selective fluorescent sensors for mercury detection in water and biological systems.

Carbon dots (CDs) have gained attention for their eco-friendliness, non-toxicity, water solubility, optical stability, and biocompatibility. Their strong fluorescence and antioxidant activity are linked to surface groups like hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amino moieties, which also confer high affinity for Hg²⁺ making CDs effective for environmental mercury detection [18]. To enhance their luminescence, various strategies have been applied, including carbon source selection, surface functionalization, and purification [19]. The carbon source critically affects CD properties, and using waste materials offers a sustainable, cost-effective route. Materials like graphene, carbon nanotubes, citric acid, sucrose, tires, chewing gum, and used frying oil have been utilized for CD synthesis [20].

Despite recycling efforts, limited research has focused on reusing waste toner powder for CD synthesis, as shown in Table 1 [21–27]. Electronic waste, including toner cartridges, is often incinerated or landfilled, causing environmental hazards such as soil contamination and toxic gas emissions [28]. Waste toner powder, comprising 8 % of a toner cartridge, contains organic compounds that are toxic and resistant to decomposition. While previous studies have explored its use in synthetic rubber, iron oxide transformation, graphene oxide hydrogels, and asphalt, large amounts of toner powder still go unused. This underscores the need for innovative recycling approaches, particularly for developing CDs from toner powder a gap this study aims to address, particularly in fingerprinting, anti-counterfeiting, and Hg²⁺ sensing applications.

Fingerprints are unique, permanent identifiers, but LFPs are often invisible and require developers to enhance contrast. The effectiveness of these developers relies on contrast, selectivity, sensitivity, and low toxicity. However, current fluorescent materials for fingerprint visualization face challenges such as weak fluorescence, complex structures, and high costs. Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) materials offer promising fluorescence but require organic solvents, while semiconductor quantum dots contain toxic heavy metals. These limitations highlight the need for safer, cost-effective alternatives. Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising candidates for fingerprint recognition due to their excellent optical properties, affordability, and low toxicity [29,30].

Counterfeit prevention is a growing concern, leading to the development of technologies like watermarks, laser holography, and photoluminescent materials. Room-temperature phosphorescent materials, known for their brightness and durability, are increasingly favoured over fluorescent materials due to their prolonged emission and resistance to light degradation. However, their high production costs, potential toxicity, and complex synthesis limit widespread use. Recent studies have incorporated photoluminescent materials into natural polymer gels, with cellulose emerging as a suitable substrate, though it lacks inherent photoluminescence. CDs, known for their excellent photoluminescence, stability, and affordability, are being explored for anti-counterfeiting applications [31–33].

This study presents a sustainable approach to recycling waste toner powder through the hydrothermal synthesis of TCDs. The synthesized TCDs were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and various optical techniques. Their selectivity and sensitivity for Hg2+ detection were evaluated through both probe-based and paper sensor applications. Additionally, TCDs were employed for fingerprint visualization, enabling the identification of Level I-III fingerprint features. Furthermore, TCD-based anti-counterfeiting tags were developed and tested for practical implementation.

Black toner powder, previously utilized in Samsung and Hewlett-Packard toner cartridges, was sourced from Tumkur university (Karnataka, India). Ethanolamine (≥98 %), anhydrous ethanol (95 %), and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All reagents were used in their original form without additional purification or modifications.

A total of 100 mg of toner powder was dispersed in 11 mL of ethanol and stirred for 10 min to ensure uniform mixing. Ethanolamine (0.01 mol) was then added to the mixture, which was transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave. The reaction proceeded at 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling, the solution underwent centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 15 min at 4 °C to separate the solid byproducts. The collected supernatant was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane filter to remove any residual particulates. Subsequently, dialysis was performed using a 2000 Da membrane with deionized water for 24 h to eliminate smaller molecular contaminants. Finally, the purified solution was freeze-dried to obtain the final TCDs, which were stored for further experimentation ( Fig. 1 a).

To examine the impact of functional groups on ion detection systems, TCDs were oxidized to produce oxidized-TCDs (o-TCDs). Specifically, 50 mg of TCDs was added to 50 mL of a 5 % peroxyacetic acid solution and incubated at 40 ℃ for 6 h. The mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24 h. The resulting o-TCDs solution was stored at 4 ℃ until use.

The fabrication of paper sensors was achieved by direct immersion of filter paper into the TCD solution. Filter paper strips measuring 1.5 × 1 cm were prepared and submerged in the TCD solution for 30 min. The strips were then carefully removed using tweezers and air-dried. The resulting fluorescent test paper strips were stored for future use in Hg2+ detection without requiring any additional treatment (Fig. 1 b).

The fluorescence quantum yield of the synthesized TCDs was determined using quinine sulfate (QS) as the reference standard, following previously established protocols. The quantum yield was calculated using the equation [34]:

Where, A represents the absorbance intensity, Ф denotes the fluorescence quantum yield, I indicate the integrated fluorescence emission intensity, η corresponds to the refractive index of the sample, st represents the standard reference quinine sulfate (QS). To minimize reabsorption effects, the absorbance of all analyzed samples was maintained below 0.1. The fluorescence quantum yield was measured in 0.1 M H₂SO₄ as the solvent for accurate calculations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ using TCD solution
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the sensitivity of TCDs for Hg2+ detection, the TCD solution was diluted to 1.5 times its original concentration. Equal volumes of this diluted solution were mixed with varying concentrations of Hg2+ ions, ranging from 0 to 500 μM (0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 3, 5, 8, 10, 30, 50, 80, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 μM). The fluorescence spectra of all prepared samples were then recorded and analysed. To investigate the selectivity of the TCD probe, fluorescence responses were measured in the presence of fifteen different metal cations, including Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Ni2+. The impact of these metal ions on the fluorescence properties of TCDs was systematically evaluated. Additionally, the fluorescence images of all samples under UV light were captured using a smartphone camera for visual comparison.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Hg2+ using TCD-based paper sensors
ce:section.ce:para: A simple and convenient approach for Hg2+ detection was developed using TCD-impregnated paper strips. These strips were immersed in the test solution for approximately 2–3 min and subsequently air-dried. The sensitivity of the paper sensor was determined by applying 20 μL of Hg2+ solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μM onto the paper strips. After natural air drying, the fluorescence images of the test strips were examined under UV light, and the results were compared to the initial fluorescence images of the untreated TCD-based paper sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of TCDs and paper sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of both the TCD solution and TCD-based paper sensors, after interaction with Hg2+, was regenerated using an EDTA-saturated solution. The fluorescence spectra of the recovered samples were analysed and compared with those of the initial blank samples. The restored TCD solution and paper sensors were subsequently reused for further Hg2+ detection cycles. For paper sensor regeneration, the used strips were submerged in an EDTA-saturated solution for 20 min. After soaking, the strips were carefully removed using tweezers and dried at room temperature. Once dried, these regenerated paper sensors were ready for reuse in subsequent Hg2+ detection experiments.

Incorporating a dispersing agent is critical for the preparation of AC ink, primarily to ensure proper viscosity and dispersion. Viscosity enhances adhesion to surfaces during printing, while uniform dispersion prevents aggregation, maintaining ink transparency. To prepare the security ink, 30 mL of deionized (DI) water was placed on a magnetic stirrer, and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was gradually dissolved in it until a clear solution was formed. Subsequently, TCDs were uniformly dispersed into this mixture, resulting in a transparent, fluorescent AC ink (Fig. 1 c).

In this approach, the fluorescent security ink containing TCDs was deposited at one end of a printing screen plate. Using a squeegee, pressure was applied to spread the ink across the plate. As the squeegee moved, the ink passed through the mesh of the patterned section, transferring the design onto the substrate. This technique enabled the printing of intricate security patterns on various materials. Once dried, the printed patterns exhibited a stable and intense blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light while remaining invisible under normal daylight conditions, thereby demonstrating their effectiveness in concealed security applications.

For this technique, the anti-counterfeiting ink was prepared by dispersing an optimized amount of TCDs into a PVA solution via sonication. The resulting ink was loaded into a sketch pen and used to manually draw or trace security patterns on various surfaces. This method provided a simple and versatile approach to integrating anti-counterfeiting features into documents, packaging, and labels.

The prepared TCD-based ink was also tested for applications in writing, drawing, and printing. The ink was applied to non-fluorescent paper using a brush, allowing for the creation of security patterns, text, or artistic designs. Images of the fluorescent ink on paper were captured under both daylight and 365 nm UV light using a Canon EOS 1500D Digital SLR Camera (EF S18–55 Lens). The developed AC patterns obtained through different methods are illustrated in Fig. 1 c.

In this experiment, LFPs were obtained from the thumb finger of a 25-year-old donor. To enhance the adherence of natural skin secretions, the donor gently rubbed their forehead or nose, ensuring the presence of grease and sweat residues on the fingertip. The fingerprint was then imprinted onto different substrate surfaces by pressing the finger firmly against them. For fingerprint visualization, TCD powder was evenly applied over the LFPs using the powder dusting technique. Excess powder was carefully removed by blowing it off, revealing the fingerprint patterns. Finally, the developed fingerprints were photographed using a Canon EOS 1500D Digital SLR Camera under 365 nm UV light, enabling clear imaging of the ridge patterns (Fig. 1 d).

To analyze the structural, optical, and surface properties of the synthesized TCDs, a series of characterization techniques were employed: X-ray Diffraction (XRD): A Shimadzu 7000 powder X-ray diffractometer was used to examine the crystalline structure of the samples. The scanning was conducted at a rate of 0.02°/min over a 2θ range of 10–60°. Raman Spectroscopy: The molecular vibrational modes were investigated using a Confocal RAMAN spectrometer (Model STR 500), equipped with a 532 nm excitation laser source. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR): FTIR spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer Frontier FTIR spectrometer (PerkinElmer, Inc., USA). Before analysis, the sample was dried at 50°C in an oven until a stable weight was achieved. Measurements were performed across the spectral range of 4000–400 cm−1. Fluorescence Spectroscopy: The fluorescence characteristics of the TCDs were analyzed using a Horiba spectrofluorometer equipped with a 450 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Fluorescence Quantum Yield (QY): The fluorescence quantum yield of TCDs was determined using a photometric integrating sphere-assisted technique along with transient steady-state spectroscopy (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM): Morphological and structural properties were examined using a JEOL 2100 HRTEM, operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS): Surface elemental composition and chemical states of the materials were analyzed via XPS measurements, performed using an ESCALAB MKII spectrometer with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray.

The SEM analysis reveals that the synthesized TCDs exhibit a spherical shape with a uniform morphology ( Fig. 2 a). While slight aggregation is observed, the particles maintain a smooth surface structure. The TEM images further confirm that the TCDs are nanosized, with diameters measuring below 10 nm (Fig. 2 b). HR-TEM imaging distinctly shows lattice fringes, with an interplanar spacing of 0.22 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon. These observations suggest that the TCDs possess a predominantly single-crystal structure [35]. Fig. 2 c presents the EDX spectrum of the TCDs, displaying prominent peaks attributed to C, O and N. The accompanying inset table provides both weight and atomic percentages of these elements, confirming that carbon is the major constituent, with smaller proportions of oxygen and nitrogen contributing to the elemental composition. To confirm the elemental composition and spatial distribution of C, O and N within the CDs, EDS elemental mapping was carried out. The elemental maps (Fig. 2 d) clearly demonstrate a uniform and homogeneous distribution of C, O and N across the entire surface of the particles. This uniformity suggests successful incorporation of these elements during the synthesis process. The absence of localized agglomeration or elemental clustering further supports the effective dispersion of the dopants and matrix elements within the nanostructure.

As depicted in Fig. 2 e, the diffraction pattern exhibits peaks at 2θ = 20.3° and 40.56°, which correspond to the (002) and (100) planes, respectively. These peaks are characteristic of the graphitic carbon phase, indicating that the prepared TCDs possess a graphite-like core structure [36]. Further structural characterization was conducted using Raman spectroscopy, with the corresponding Raman spectrum shown in Fig. 2 f. The spectrum of the synthesized TCDs exhibits two broad peaks centered at 1379 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1. The peak at 1379 cm−1, identified as the D-band, is associated with structural defects and disordered graphitic regions. Meanwhile, the peak at 1596 cm−1, known as the G-band, corresponds to the CC in-plane stretching vibration (E2 g mode) of graphitic carbon structures [37]. The presence of these peaks confirms the partially graphitic nature of the TCDs and suggests a balance between sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon domains.

Fig. 3 a presents the FT-IR spectra of both pristine toner powder and the synthesized TCDs, revealing characteristic absorption peaks. A peak at 2942 cm−1, observed in both samples, corresponds to the stretching vibrations of –CH bonds. The presence of CO bonds in both samples is indicated by the absorption peak at 1709 cm−1 [38]. The absorption band at 1603 cm−1 results from the overlapping in-plane stretching vibrations of aromatic carbon, while peaks between 1509 and 1462 cm−1 correspond to C–C stretching in aromatic rings and azo bonding. Additionally, a peak at 1245 cm−1 is attributed to C–O stretching vibrations, while peaks at 1174 and 1090 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching of C–O–C and C–OH bonds, respectively. The absorption peak at 1019 cm−1 originates from in-plane –CH vibrations, while the characteristic peak at 822 cm−1 is linked to the mono-substituted C–O–C bond stretching. For the waste toner powder, additional absorption peaks at 758 and 688 cm−1 were observed, corresponding to out-of-plane –CH vibrations. In contrast, the FT-IR spectrum of TCDs displayed a new absorption peak at 737 cm−1, associated with the stretching of aliphatic –CH₂ bonds. A comparative analysis of both spectra indicates that, while the –CH bonds exhibited a slight reduction in intensity, the characteristic absorptions of C–O bonds in the TCDs increased, suggesting enhanced oxygen functionalization [39]. The FTIR spectra of toner powder and TCDs, along with their deconvoluted profiles, are presented in Figure S1 & S2. The corresponding functional group assignments derived from the deconvoluted FTIR spectra are summarized in Table S1 [40].

To further investigate the elemental composition and chemical properties of the TCDs, XPS analysis was conducted. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3 b) confirms the presence of C, N, and O without any detectable impurity peaks. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3 c) exhibits three distinct peaks at 284.3 eV, 286.1 eV, and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O, and CO bonds, respectively [41,42]. Similarly, the O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3 d) displays two deconvoluted peaks at 532.3 eV and 534.2 eV, attributed to CO and C–O bonds, respectively [43]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3 e) reveals three peaks at 401.9 eV, 400.8 eV, and 399.8 eV, assigned to CN, C–N, and N–H bonds, respectively. The XPS data further corroborate the FT-IR results, confirming the successful incorporation of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups in the synthesized TCDs.

The UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized TCDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. A weak absorption peak at 350 nm is attributed to n → π electronic transitions* arising from C–O and C–C conjugation. The primary fluorescence emission band of the TCDs appears at 445 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The corresponding CIE color coordinate diagram indicates a cyan-blue emission at (0.197, 0.212) (Fig. 4 b). Excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) is a well-known characteristic of fluorescent carbon-based materials, particularly carbon dots. Among their optical features, PL remains one of the most intriguing properties. To investigate this, the fluorescence spectra of the TCDs were recorded by varying the excitation wavelength from 305 to 395 nm in 10 nm increments. All TCDs exhibited a clear excitation-dependent emission behavior [44], consistent with findings reported in previous studies. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission spectra exhibited a redshift (bathochromic shift) [45], as shown in Fig. 4 c. The optimal excitation wavelength for TCDs was found to be 365 nm, corresponding to an emission peak at 445 nm. Therefore, 365 nm was selected as the most suitable wavelength for further analysis. This excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior can be attributed to the presence of various surface energy traps on the TCDs. Additionally, the observed shift in emission peak position is likely influenced by the size distribution of the TCDs. The fluorescence QY of the synthesized CDs was determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H₂SO₄ as a standard reference, which has a known QY of 0.54 under excitation at 360 nm. The QY was calculated using the Eq. 1. To ensure accurate results, absorbance values at 360 nm were maintained below 0.1 to minimize reabsorption effects. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under identical experimental conditions (Fig. 4 d). The quantum yield of the CDs was found to be 22.3 %, indicating their moderate to strong fluorescence efficiency [46]. This high QY can be attributed to the presence of surface functional groups and efficient passivation, which suppress non-radiative recombination pathways.

The photostability and environmental responsiveness of the synthesized TCDs were thoroughly investigated under various conditions, including ageing, UV irradiation, and pH variation, as illustrated in Figure S3. The ageing effect on the fluorescence properties of TCDs was assessed by monitoring their emission spectra over a series of days. As shown in Figure S3a, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased over time, indicating a decline in photoluminescence with prolonged storage. This observation is further supported by the normalized intensity graph (Figure S3b) and corresponding digital photographs (Figure S3c), which display a noticeable reduction in blue fluorescence upon visual inspection. To understand the effect of light exposure, the TCDs and their o-TCDs were subjected to UV irradiation for varying durations. o-TCDs exhibited reduced photostability, retaining only 78.3 % of their fluorescence after 120 minutes of UV irradiation, compared to 89.6 % retention by the unmodified TCDs under the same conditions (Figure S3 (d-f) & (g-i)). Additionally, the fluorescence QY of o-TCDs was lower (19.3 %) than that of the unmodified TCDs (22.3 %), which could adversely affect the long-term sensitivity and reproducibility of the sensing system (Fig. 4 d). Furthermore, o-TCDs displayed broader emission profiles and less defined surface functionalities, potentially diminishing selectivity in complex analytical environments. Additionally, the effect of pH on the optical properties of TCDs was examined across a broad pH range. At pH 1, fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched, and a similar effect was observed under highly alkaline conditions at pH 14. However, the maximum fluorescence intensity was achieved at pH 7, closely resembling physiological conditions (Figure S3j). This trend was corroborated by the bar graph of normalized intensities (Figure S3k) and the visual appearance of fluorescence under UV light (Figure S3l). These results collectively confirm that the TCDs exhibit considerable environmental sensitivity, with their photoluminescent behavior being strongly influenced by storage duration, UV exposure, and pH, making them suitable for potential applications in sensing and bioimaging.
These results confirm the stability of TCDs under neutral conditions, making them highly suitable for sensing applications, forensic fingerprint detection, and anti-counterfeiting technologies [47].

To evaluate the selectivity of TCDs and their corresponding paper sensors for Hg2+ detection, fluorescence responses were tested against a series of cationic solutions (100 μM), including Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ba2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Cr6+, Mg2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, and Ni2+ ( Fig. 5 a). The results demonstrated a distinct fluorescence quenching effect in the presence of Hg2+, whereas the fluorescence intensities remained largely unchanged for other metal ions under identical conditions. This suggests that TCDs possess excellent selectivity for Hg2+ ions. To further validate specificity, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) was calculated, where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the TCD solution in the absence and presence of Hg2+, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 5 b, the F/F0 value for Hg2+ exhibited a significant decrease, confirming the high selectivity of TCDs toward Hg2+ ions compared to other cations. Visual fluorescence images of TCDs solutions under UV light (Fig. 5 c) further confirmed this selectivity, as Hg2+ exposure led to a drastic reduction in fluorescence, whereas no apparent changes were observed with other ions. A similar pattern was observed for TCD-based paper sensors (Fig. 5 d). The bright blue fluorescence of the paper sensors was effectively quenched upon exposure to Hg2+ solutions, while no visible fluorescence reduction was noted with other cationic solutions. These findings establish the strong specificity of TCDs and their paper sensors for Hg2+ detection [48].

To assess potential interference from coexisting metal ions, an interference test was conducted, where Hg2+ detection was performed in the presence of other cations (Figure S4a). The results (Figure S4b) revealed that fluorescence quenching by Hg2+ remained unaffected by the presence of competing metal ions, confirming that the designed sensor exhibits high resistance to interference. These observations suggest that the developed TCDs provide a reliable and selective detection platform for Hg2+ ions, even in complex environmental and biological samples [49].

Following the confirmation of selectivity, further studies were conducted to evaluate the efficiency and sensitivity of TCDs for Hg2+ detection. The relationship between fluorescence quenching and Hg2+ concentration plays a crucial role in determining detection efficiency. Sensitivity is a key parameter in assessing the detection limit of the system, and achieving rapid, high-sensitivity, and real-time monitoring of heavy metal ions is essential for practical applications. To determine the detection limit, solutions with varying Hg2+ concentrations (0.001–500 μM) were prepared and mixed with TCDs, followed by shaking for 20 min before fluorescence measurements were recorded. The fluorescence response curve is presented in Fig. 5 e. To understand the quenching mechanism of the synthesized TCDs in the presence of Hg²⁺ ions, a Stern–Volmer (S–V) analysis was conducted [50,51]. The quenching efficiency was evaluated by plotting the ratio of fluorescence intensities F/F0 against the concentration of Hg²⁺ ions (Fig. 5 f). The S–V plot exhibited two distinct linear regions: from 0.001 to 200 μM, the relationship was described by the equation F/F0= 0.96 −0.0026 C (R² = 0.99), while in the higher concentration range of 200–500 μM, the plot followed F/F0= 0.64 −0.0009 C (R² = 0.99). This biphasic linearity suggests the involvement of two different quenching behaviors depending on the quencher concentration. Additionally, at lower concentrations (0.001–200 μM), the quenching is more efficient (steeper slope), possibly due to the high availability of accessible binding sites on the carbon dot surface. As the concentration increases beyond 200 μM, a decrease in quenching efficiency is observed, which may be attributed to saturation of active sites and/or formation of aggregates that limit further complexation. The LOD was calculated to be 17.03 nM using the 3σ/k method, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank and k is the slope from the linear region (0–200 μM). Compared to previously reported Hg²⁺ sensors ( Table 2), the developed TCD-based probe demonstrated a broader detection range and a lower LOD, underscoring its enhanced sensitivity and practical applicability in trace-level mercury detection [52–61].

Importantly, time-resolved fluorescence measurements revealed that the lifetime of the TCDs remained nearly unchanged after the addition of Hg²⁺ (from 6.17 ns to 6.09 ns), indicating that the quenching mechanism is predominantly static. In static quenching, fluorophores form non-fluorescent ground-state complexes with quenchers, which reduces emission intensity without affecting the excited-state lifetime. This conclusion is further supported by the deviation from linearity across a broad concentration range, often indicative of ground-state complex formation, as well as by the significant difference in slope between the low and high concentration regimes. Furthermore, in dynamic quenching, the S-V plot is typically linear across the entire concentration range and shows a direct relationship with decreasing lifetime. In contrast, the deviation in slope between the two concentration ranges in this study, along with the unchanged lifetime, clearly supports the predominance of static quenching. The reduced quenching efficiency at higher concentrations (shallower slope from 200–500 μM) may also indicate saturation of binding sites or the formation of aggregates that hinder further complexation. Furthermore, fluorescence images of the TCD-based paper sensor demonstrated that as the Hg2+ concentration increased, the fluorescence intensity progressively diminished, consistent with the fluorescence emission spectra. This observation confirms the strong reproducibility and reliability of the TCD-based paper sensor as a visual detection platform for Hg2+ (Fig. 5 g & h).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of the smartphone-assisted visual analysis platform
ce:section.ce:para: An important observation from Fig. 5 g is the significant color change of the TCD probe under 365 nm UV light, corresponding to increasing Hg2+ concentrations (0.001–500 μM). Building on this visual response, a smartphone-assisted visual detection method for Hg2+ was developed. To implement this method, fluorescence images of TCDs at different Hg2+ concentrations (0.001–500 μM) were captured using a OnePlus Nord CE 3 Lite smartphone camera under a portable UV lamp in a dark environment. The extracted RGB values were processed using the Color Identification app, where R, G, and B correspond to red, green, and blue channels, respectively [62]. The data revealed that as the Hg2+ concentration increased, the B-values (blue intensity) gradually decreased. A linear relationship was observed when plotting the R/B ratio against Hg2+ concentration, as shown in Figure S5a. The fitted linear regression equation for the liquid-phase probe was: y = 0.0038x+ 0.0066 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.994, confirming the strong linearity of the response. The LOD for this smartphone-assisted method was calculated as 115.3 nM. These findings demonstrate that the TCD-based smartphone-assisted platform provides a highly sensitive, rapid, and portable solution for Hg2+ detection, making it ideal for on-site environmental monitoring in emergency pollution scenarios [63].

To further evaluate the detection sensitivity of the TCD-based paper sensor, paper strips were immersed in solutions containing Hg2+ (0–100 μM). Under 365 nm UV light, the fluorescence intensity of the paper sensor gradually diminished as Hg2+ concentration increased (Fig. 5 f), mirroring the color changes observed in the liquid-phase TCD probe. The clear fluorescence shift under UV light enabled semi-quantitative visual detection, making the sensor suitable for practical field applications. To enhance accuracy and quantitative analysis, smartphone-based image processing was applied. Similar to the liquid-phase detection, the RGB values were extracted from the images, and the B-values decreased progressively with increasing Hg2+ concentration. The R/B ratio exhibited a strong linear relationship with Hg2+ concentration, following the equation: y = 0.0025x+ 0.017, with a high correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998, indicating exceptional detection accuracy (Figure S5b). The LOD for the paper-based sensor was determined to be 180 nM. These results confirm that the smartphone-assisted TCD-based paper sensor platform is an effective, portable, and intelligent tool for the quantitative detection of Hg2+. This approach not only provides a straightforward and efficient strategy for monitoring Hg2+ pollution but also inspires advancements in rapid, on-site, point-of-care testing methodologies. The integration of TCDs with smartphone-based image processing paves the way for future developments in digital environmental monitoring and real-time heavy metal detection [64].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic gate and reversible fluorescence behaviour
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence recovery of TCDs following treatment with an EDTA solution highlights their reversible detection capability. As demonstrated in Fig. 6 a, TCDs exhibited stable fluorescence quenching upon Hg2+ addition, which was subsequently restored by EDTA treatment. This cycle of fluorescence suppression and reactivation could be repeated up to four times, with negligible loss in fluorescence efficiency. The fluorescence recovery rate exceeded 95 %, as shown in Fig. 6 b, confirming the robustness of this system. A similar fluorescence recovery behaviour was also observed in TCD-based paper sensors, demonstrating that both the solution and solid-phase sensors are reusable, enhancing their sustainability. To further illustrate the detection mechanism and its response, a logic gate model was constructed. As depicted in Fig. 6 c & d, the TCD detection system follows an "OR" logic gate with two input states: Input 1: Presence of Hg2+ (quenches fluorescence), Input 2: Addition of EDTA (restores fluorescence), Output: Fluorescence signal (ON = 1, OFF = 0), In the presence of Hg2+ alone (Input = 1, Output = 0), fluorescence was quenched. However, when EDTA was introduced (Input = 1, 1), fluorescence emission at 445 nm was restored (Output = 1). This switching behaviour establishes the TCDs-based system as a reversible and reusable fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection [65].

Fluorescence quenching can occur through multiple mechanisms, including static and dynamic quenching, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), surface energy transfer (SET), Dexter energy transfer (DET), and photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [66]. To determine the specific mechanism responsible for Hg2+ detection in this system, several analytical techniques, including UV-Vis absorption, FTIR spectroscopy, and fluorescence lifetime measurements, were employed. As shown in Fig. 7 a, no spectral overlap was observed between the absorption spectrum of Hg2+ and the excitation/emission spectra of TCDs, ruling out inner filter effects (IFE) and FRET as possible quenching mechanisms [67]. Interestingly, the characteristic TCDs absorption peak at 241 nm significantly diminished after Hg2+ introduction (Fig. 7 b), suggesting the formation of non-fluorescent complexes, indicative of static quenching [68].

The optical properties of the TCDs were further investigated using fluorescence (FL) lifetime measurements. The FL decay profile of the TCDs was precisely fitted using a tri-exponential function, as shown below [69]:

In this analysis, the photoluminescence (PL) decay function Y(t) demonstrates time-dependent behavior characterized by three distinct lifetime components: T1, T2, and T3. Each of these lifetimes contributes to the overall decay profile with corresponding fractional amplitudes A1, A2 and A3 respectively. The decay curve is presented in Fig. 7 c, with the inset providing a detailed view of the individual decay regions, highlighting the contributions of each component to the overall PL decay process. The observed exponential decay behavior suggests the presence of three distinct emissive sites on the surface of the TCDs. Among these, T1 corresponds to the lifetime associated with direct electron–hole recombination, whereas T2 and T3 represent longer lifetimes attributed to the delayed relaxation of excitons to lower energy states. The tri-exponential fitting of the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves yielded a superior correlation coefficient (Rtri 2 =0.999) compared to the bi-exponential fit (Rbi 2 =0.998), confirming the necessity of three decay components. The average lifetimes obtained for the three emissive sites were (2.483 ± 2.725) ns, (2.484 ± 3.300) ns, and (11.345 ± 1.876) ns, respectively, further supporting the existence of multiple relaxation pathways contributing to the photoluminescence of the TCDs. In contrast, the average fluorescence lifetimes of TCDs and TCDs in the presence of Hg²⁺ were measured to be 6.17 and 6.09 ns, respectively. These values are consistent with previously reported findings in the literature [70]. Notably, the negligible change in lifetime following the addition of Hg²⁺ suggests that the quenching mechanism is predominantly static in nature. This is because static quenching involves the formation of non-emissive ground-state complexes, which reduces fluorescence intensity without altering the excited-state lifetime unlike dynamic quenching, which typically results in a measurable decrease in lifetime [71,72].

Previous studies suggest that the interaction between oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl) and Hg2+ plays a critical role in fluorescence quenching [73–75]. To further validate this, FTIR spectroscopy was performed. As shown in Fig. 7 d, the C–O and CO peaks significantly decreased upon the addition of Hg2+, indicating direct interaction between these functional groups and Hg2+ ions [76]. These groups likely coordinate with Hg2+, forming stable complexes, which contribute to fluorescence quenching.

To further investigate the relationship between Hg2+ quenching and oxygen functional groups, o-TCDs with an increased concentration of oxygen-containing groups were synthesized. Compared to regular TCDs, o-TCDs exhibited significantly higher fluorescence quenching efficiency upon Hg2+ exposure, increasing from 74.64 % to 90.14 % at 500 μM Hg2+ (Fig. 7 e). XPS data for o-TCDs (Figure S6a -d) confirmed an increased proportion of oxygen functional groups, particularly carboxyl groups, which rose from 4.46 % to 19.79 %. This significant increase in oxygen-containing moieties likely enhanced the interaction between o-TCDs and Hg2+, leading to stronger quenching behaviour (Fig. 7 f). Based on these findings, a schematic representation of the Hg2+ sensing mechanism is presented in Fig. 7 g. The key steps are Fluorescence Quenching (TCDs + Hg2+): TCDs contain oxygen-rich functional groups (C–O, CO, and –OH) that serve as primary binding sites for Hg2+. Upon binding, TCDs and Hg2+ form a non-fluorescent ground-state complex, leading to fluorescence quenching (static quenching mechanism). Second step is Fluorescence Recovery (Addition of EDTA): The introduction of EDTA, a strong chelating agent, displaces Hg2+ from the TCDs-Hg2+ complex. This removes the quenching effect, allowing fluorescence emission to recover. This reversible interaction underpins the reusability of TCDs and paper sensors for Hg2+ detection, further demonstrating their sustainable and efficient sensing performance. Overall, the study confirms that the fluorescence quenching of TCDs by Hg2+ follows a static quenching mechanism, primarily driven by oxygen-containing functional groups on the TCD surface. The reversibility of fluorescence quenching and restoration using EDTA establishes this system as a sustainable and reusable sensing platform. Moreover, the integration of a logic gate model provides an intuitive representation of the detection process, reinforcing the practical applicability of this system in environmental monitoring, forensic analysis, and anti-counterfeiting applications.

To evaluate the versatility and applicability of the synthesized TCD-based security ink, various printing techniques were employed, including screen printing, sketching with a pen, and brush application [77,78]. Fig. 8 a displays anti-counterfeiting labels fabricated using these techniques, demonstrating the adaptability of the ink across different substrates and application methods. The images in Fig. 8 b highlight the labels appearance under UV light (365 nm). While the labels remain invisible or faintly visible under normal lighting, exposure to 365 nm UV light induces a cyan-blue fluorescence emission, making the encrypted patterns and security markings distinctly visible. This fluorescence response offers a robust and easily detectable anti-counterfeiting solution, significantly enhancing security against counterfeit attempts. The synthesized TCDs exhibit excellent solubility and dispersibility within a PVA matrix, ensuring their effective integration into security inks. Their strong fluorescence properties and stability make them highly suitable for anti-counterfeiting markers, providing an efficient and cost-effective approach to secure labelling [79]. Among the different application techniques, the brush mode was found to be particularly advantageous for creating intricate and detailed security patterns. Compared to screen printing and sketching with a pen, the brush technique allows for greater precision, ease of application, and cost-efficiency. This makes it an ideal choice for producing customized security patterns or encrypted markings, expanding the potential applications of TCD-based security inks across various industries [80].

To examine the longevity of the fluorescent security labels, aging experiments were conducted for up to 200 days under room temperature (RT) conditions (Fig. 8 c & c
1 ). The results indicate that the fluorescent AC labels on paper exhibited a vibrant blue glow under UV (365 nm) light for up to 100 days. However, after this period, the fluorescence gradually decreased, signifying a reduction in luminescence stability over extended aging. Despite this, the fluorescence was still well-retained beyond 100 days, demonstrating strong long-term stability (Figure S7a). Further stability assessments were carried out under ultrasonication treatment to evaluate the ink's mechanical durability (Fig. 8 d & d
1 ). The AC labels were immersed in a beaker and subjected to ultrasonication for up to 120 min, with fluorescence intensity measurements recorded at 30 min intervals. The fluorescence remained highly stable for the first 60 min, after which a gradual decline was observed (Figure S7b). Even after 120 min of ultrasonic treatment, the fluorescence intensity remained at 84.7 %, indicating the strong mechanical robustness of the TCD-based AC labels. These findings reinforce the practical usability of TCD-based security inks, highlighting their durability, stability, and multifunctionality. The robust optical properties and long storage stability make them an ideal candidate for secure labelling, authentication, and counterfeit prevention across a diverse range of industries [81,82].

The effectiveness of a fingerprint powder is largely determined by its ability to restore Level 1–3 ridge features, which include overall fingerprint patterns, minutiae points, and pore structures [83,84]. However, LFPs are often deposited on surfaces with varying textures, posing a challenge for accurate visualization. As shown in Fig. 9 a, fingerprints developed on engineered wood flooring exhibit reduced clarity under UV light due to the complex surface texture. Despite this, the contrast between ridges and furrows remains distinguishable in the developed fingerprint. This is further validated using pixel profile analysis (Figure 9a
1 ), which highlights the intensity variations across the fingerprint ridges and furrows. Although uniformity is not maintained likely due to the porous nature of the surface the differentiation between ridges and furrows remains apparent. To further evaluate the applicability of TCDs on porous surfaces, a 3D interactive surface plot was generated for the core region of the fingerprint (Figure 9a
2 ). This visualization confirms the non-uniform distribution of TCDs on the porous substrate while maintaining ridge and furrow differentiation [85,86]. To investigate the performance of TCDs on semi-porous surfaces, fingerprints were developed on a leather belt (Fig. 9 b). Since fingerprint sharpness depends on grayscale contrast between ridges and furrows, a pixel profile was generated, revealing high grayscale differentiation and uniformity. The 3D surface plot (Figure 9b
1
& b
2 ) further confirms that TCDs effectively highlight fingerprint patterns on semi-porous surfaces, reinforcing their applicability for forensic fingerprint detection. In addition to porous and semi-porous substrates, TCDs were also tested on non-porous surfaces, such as a stainless-steel sheet (Fig. 9 c). The developed fingerprint exhibits continuous, sharp, and well-defined ridges, while minimal TCD adhesion to the background ensures high contrast. This confirms that TCDs demonstrate high selectivity and sensitivity in fingerprint development on non-porous materials (Figure 9c
1
& c
2 ).

Fingerprint analysis is typically categorized into three levels: Level 1 features: Overall fingerprint patterns (loops, whorls, arches). Level 2 features: Minutiae points such as bifurcations, ridge endings, islands, and enclosures. Level 3 features: Fine details such as pores. As illustrated in Fig. 9 d, the developed fingerprints reveal clear loop patterns, representing Level 1 fingerprint features. Additionally, several Level 2 minutiae points were identified, highlighted in red lines and numbered, including: Double bifurcation (No. 3), Short ridge (No. 4), Eye (No. 5), Island (No. 6), Enclosure (No. 7), Ridge endings (No. 8), Bifurcation (No. 9), Hook (No. 10), Dot (No. 11). Furthermore, enlarged views (No. 12) reveal Level 3 features, specifically pores, which are clearly visible in the developed fingerprint. These observations confirm that TCDs are highly effective in restoring Level 1–3 fingerprint features, making them an ideal candidate for forensic fingerprint development [87].

To evaluate the long-term stability of fingerprints developed with TCDs, samples were stored at room temperature for 120 days (Fig. 9 e). Poroscopy analysis revealed that pore structures remained clearly visible up to 60 days. However, by day 120, distinguishing pores along the ridges became challenging, although the ridge-furrow distinction remained evident. The pixel profile analysis (Figure 9e
1 ) demonstrates that while grayscale values decreased over time, the differentiation between ridges and furrows persisted. This confirms that TCDs maintain high contrast and clarity over extended periods, ensuring their suitability for forensic applications. The observed photostability further supports the potential of TCDs as fingerprint powders in forensic science [88–90]. The findings demonstrate that TCDs effectively restore fingerprint details across various substrates, including porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces. The high sensitivity and selectivity of TCDs enable the clear visualization of Level 1–3 fingerprint features, including minutiae points and pore structures. Additionally, long-term photostability tests confirm that fingerprints developed with TCDs remain detectable for extended periods, solidifying their potential for real-world forensic applications.

This study successfully synthesized fluorescent TCDs emitting cyan-blue fluorescence through a hydrothermal approach using waste toner powder. The developed TCDs demonstrated selective fluorescence quenching upon exposure to Hg2+ ions, which could be fully restored to the initial fluorescence state by adding an EDTA-saturated solution. The TCD-based sensor exhibited exceptional linearity across a wide Hg2+ detection range (0–500 µM), achieving a LOD of 17.3 nM. Additionally, the TCD probe showed a highly linear response at l concentrations (0–100 nM), with an LOD of 115.3 nM, confirming its ultrasensitive detection capabilities. To enhance practical applicability, the results were extended to a portable, cost-effective, and recyclable TCD-based paper sensor. This sensor allowed naked-eye detection of Hg2+, and when integrated with a smartphone-assisted real-time color analysis platform, it enabled quantitative detection of Hg2+ within the range of 0–50 µM, with an LOD of 180 nM. The paper sensor was reusable for at least four detection cycles, supporting resource conservation and environmental sustainability. Furthermore, this study highlights the critical role of oxygen-containing functional groups in enhancing the sensitivity of mercury ion detection. Owing to their high fluorescence quantum efficiency and "invisible" fluorescence properties, TCDs were successfully employed as fluorescent invisible ink for anti-counterfeiting applications. Security images were developed using screen printing, brush mode, and pen mode across various substrates, confirming their versatility and effectiveness. Additionally, the LFP imaging capability of TCDs was evaluated on porous, semi-porous, and non-porous surfaces, yielding sharp and well-defined Level 1–3 fingerprint details. The strong sensitivity, selectivity, and rapid response of TCDs confirm their potential as a high-performance fluorescence sensor for Hg2+ detection, anti-counterfeiting applications, and forensic fingerprint analysis. Thus, the TCD-based sensor system presents an innovative, sustainable, and multifunctional solution for heavy metal ion detection, forensic applications, and security labelling, demonstrating broad applicability in environmental monitoring and advanced material development.
